coredata.dc:title: Development of active packaging material based on polycaprolactone/hydroxypropyl methylcellulose nanofibers containing carbon dot nanoparticles for meat preservation

coredata.dc:description: This work demonstrates the feasibility of using a combination of biodegradable polycaprolactone (PCL) and natural-based hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) polymers containing carbon dots (CDs) for food packaging applications. CDs were synthesized using the insoluble portion of Zedo gum. The 5% and 15% concentrations of bare Zedo CDs (Z-CD) and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (TZ-CD) were chosen for incorporation into PCL/HPMC nanofibers (NFs). The PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% demonstrated improved tensile strength (12.88 and 15.01 MPa), water solubility (30.20 and 42.36%), and water vapor transmittance (0.62 and 0.71 g mm/m2 h kPa)), respectively. When comparing the release of CDs in 10%, 50%, and 95% ethanolic solutions, it was found that TZ-CD released more rapidly than Z-CD from NFs in all three simulants. The CDs-added NFs exhibited potent antioxidant activity (80.5%). More significant antimicrobial activity against S. aureus was observed compared to E. coli. Additionally, reductions in total volatile basic nitrogen and thiobarbituric acid values indicated that beef covered with PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% NFs had an extended shelf life of more than five days. In conclusion, PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% NFs can be used as promising biodegradable packaging materials to enhance the shelf life of meat products.

head.ce:title: Development of active packaging material based on polycaprolactone/hydroxypropyl methylcellulose nanofibers containing carbon dot nanoparticles for meat preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work demonstrates the feasibility of using a combination of biodegradable polycaprolactone (PCL) and natural-based hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC) polymers containing carbon dots (CDs) for food packaging applications. CDs were synthesized using the insoluble portion of Zedo gum. The 5% and 15% concentrations of bare Zedo CDs (Z-CD) and nitrogen and sulfur co-doped CDs (TZ-CD) were chosen for incorporation into PCL/HPMC nanofibers (NFs). The PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% demonstrated improved tensile strength (12.88 and 15.01 MPa), water solubility (30.20 and 42.36%), and water vapor transmittance (0.62 and 0.71 g mm/m2 h kPa)), respectively. When comparing the release of CDs in 10%, 50%, and 95% ethanolic solutions, it was found that TZ-CD released more rapidly than Z-CD from NFs in all three simulants. The CDs-added NFs exhibited potent antioxidant activity (80.5%). More significant antimicrobial activity against S. aureus was observed compared to E. coli. Additionally, reductions in total volatile basic nitrogen and thiobarbituric acid values indicated that beef covered with PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% NFs had an extended shelf life of more than five days. In conclusion, PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% NFs can be used as promising biodegradable packaging materials to enhance the shelf life of meat products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology has rapidly emerged as a preeminent approach in food packaging. It primarily incorporates nanoparticles into packaging materials, enhancing food product safety and quality (Moradi, Molaei, Kousheh, T. Guimarães, & McClements, 2021). Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant attention as potential candidates due to their remarkable antibacterial, antioxidant, and UV barrier properties (Moradi et al., 2021; Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2022). These nanomaterials, typically less than 10 nm in size, predominantly consist of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen elements (Koutamehr et al., 2023). Their advantages include low toxicity, thermal stability, chemical inertness, high water solubility, and simple functionalization. Nitrogen and sulfur doping produce N- and S-doped CDs, respectively, with superior physical and chemical characteristics (Kurian & Paul, 2021). The selection of dopants depends on their safety, cost-effectiveness, low toxicity, and biodegradability (Ezati et al., 2022). Various methods for carbon dot synthesis have been developed, including hydrothermal carbonization, ultrasonication, chemical oxidation, microwave-based synthesis, laser ablation, solvothermal methods, thermolysis, atmospheric plasma-based synthesis, and electrochemical approaches (Kurian & Paul, 2021). Microwave and hydrothermal methods are especially suited for mass production, offering uniform particle size, simple equipment, high yield, and controllability (Sun, Zhang, Bhandari, & Yang, 2020).

Polysaccharides are exceptionally suited as a carbon source for carbon dot synthesis due to their widespread availability, notable biocompatibility, cost-effectiveness, and diverse functional groups (Bajpai, D'Souza, & Suhail, 2019). Zedo gum, also recognized as Persian gum or Angum gum, is an anionic gum extracted from wild almonds. This gum exhibits limited water solubility: 30% w/w dissolves in cold water (SF), and 70% w/w remains water-insoluble but can partially dissolve in hot water (IF) (Dabestani, Kadkhodaee, Phillips, & Abbasi, 2018). Zedo gum's composition is primarily carbohydrates (80–90%) and uronic acid (around 10%), with galactose, arabinose, and rhamnose as the primary monosaccharides (Allafchian, Masmouei, & Jalali, 2022). The insoluble fraction contains lower amounts of nitrogen, fat, and protein than the soluble fraction (Dabestani et al., 2018). Although the soluble fraction of Zedo gum is traditionally used as a stabilizer and emulsifier, its insoluble fraction has not yet been reported in the literature as a carbon dot source.

Electrospinning technology is a valuable method for fabricating polymer-based nanofibers (NFs), adept at incorporating and immobilizing active compounds within their structure (Beikzadeh et al., 2021). This encapsulation technique is developing from the single-fluid blending process (Duan et al., 2024) to coaxial (Huang, Jiang, Zhou, Yu, & Liu, 2022), triaxial (M. Wang et al., 2020), side-by-side (Y. Wang, Yu, Liu, & Liu, 2022), and other multiple-fluid processes (W. Yu et al., 2023), for creating nanostructures, including core-shell (Shi et al., 2023), Janus (Lv et al., 2023), and their combinations (Zhou et al., 2023). These complex structures are powerful for developing novel functional materials (D.-G. Yu & Zhou, 2023). On the other hand, the encapsulation of functional ingredients in blends of natural and synthetic polymers represents one of the most facile methods to enhance the functional performances of electrospun NFs (Licona-Aguilar et al., 2023; D.-G. Yu & Zhao, 2022).

Hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC), recognized for its hydrophilic properties, is a widely-used cellulose derivative that demonstrates excellent film-forming properties (Aman Mohammadi et al., 2021; Aydogdu, Sumnu, & Sahin, 2019; Aydogdu, Yildiz, et al., 2019; Klangmuang & Sothornvit, 2016b). Owing to its abundance, cost-effectiveness, high transparency, flexibility, biodegradability, biocompatibility, and non-toxicity, HPMC is an excellent hydrocolloid biopolymer for food packaging applications (Aydogdu, Yildiz, et al., 2019; Bigi, Haghighi, Siesler, Licciardello, & Pulvirenti, 2021; Klangmuang & Sothornvit, 2016a; Moghimi, Aliahmadi, & Rafati, 2017). Moreover, HPMC NFs are utilized in the food industry to encapsulate and release antimicrobial compounds efficiently; they swell upon contact with water, facilitating the release of the antimicrobial agent (Bodbodak, Shahabi, Mohammadi, Ghorbani, & Pezeshki, 2021). Nevertheless, combining HPMC with synthetic polymers is essential to overcome the relatively low strength of fibers formed solely from natural-based polymers and the associated handling challenges (Aman Mohammadi et al., 2021). HPMC has been used in combination with natural or synthetic polymers such as ethylcellulose (Yavari & Ghorbani, 2021), polylactic acid (Bodbodak et al., 2021), and chitosan (Bigi et al., 2021). Polycaprolactone (PCL), a hydrophobic semi-crystalline polymer, is distinguished by its biodegradability, and biocompatibility (Beikzadeh et al., 2021). PCL emerges as a promising candidate for food packaging, attributing to its remarkable mechanical properties, non-toxicity, flexibility, and thermal stability (Beikzadeh et al., 2021; Choi, Chang, Kim, & Han, 2021; Malik, 2022).

Numerous studies have reported the antimicrobial and antioxidant efficacies of CD-loaded active packaging in preserving food (Ezati et al., 2022; Khan, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023; Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, & Ghasempour, 2023). To the best of our knowledge, no published study has reported the fabrication of combined NFs made from biodegradable PCL and natural-based HPMC loaded with CDs for food packaging applications. PCL, a hydrophobic polymer, when combined with hydrophilic HPMC, facilitates the release of CDs. In this study, novel carbon dot nanoparticles were synthesized from a sediment fraction of Zedo gum, considered waste, using a hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs were modulated by thiourea to obtain N S-co-doped CDs, respectively, and their properties were investigated. The present study investigates electrospun PCL/HPMC NFs containing CDs for meat preservation. Their cytotoxicity, antibacterial, and antioxidant activities were assessed, and the developed system was evaluated for beef fillet packaging applications.

Zedo gum was procured from a local market in Tabriz, Iran. Magnesium oxide (MgO), malondialdehyde (MDA), boric acid (H3BO3), ethanol, and thiobarbituric acid were obtained from Merck Co. (Germany). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetic acid (AA), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), polycaprolactone (PCL), hydroxypropyl methylcellulose (HPMC), hydrochloric acid (HCl), 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), Baird-Parker agar, Muller Hinton Agar (MHA), Mueller-Hinton broth (MHB), violet red bile agar (VRB), and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich in St. Louis, USA. Bacterial strains Escherichia coli (PTCC-1270) and Staphylococcus aureus (PTCC-1015) were obtained from the Pasteur Institute of Iran.

At first, Zedo gum was dispersed in water (2%) and stirred at 70 °C for 2 h. The insoluble part of Zedo gum was separated from the soluble fraction by a centrifuge at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Subsequently, the sediment was dried in an oven (Fater Electronic Co., Iran) at 60 °C. Finally, the dried sample was powdered in a porcelain mortar.

Zedo carbon dots (Z-CD) were produced by blending 3 g of the powdered insoluble fraction of Zedo gum with 150 mL of distilled water through the hydrothermal method. N, S-co-doped CDs were synthesized by adding 0.57 g of thiourea (TZ-CD) to the mentioned dispersion. These solutions underwent sonication for 15 min before being transferred to a 200 mL autoclave reactor. They were then heated for 5 h at 180 °C. After cooling to 22 °C, the resulting solutions were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, followed by filtration using a 0.22 μm syringe filter and 20 min of sonication in an ultrasonic bath (Elmasonic S60H model, Singen, Germany). Finally, the prepared solutions were freeze-dried using a freeze dryer (Epsilon 1–4 LSCplus, Martin Christ, Osterode, Germany) (Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, & Ghasempour, 2023).

A spectrofluorometer (FP-750, Jasco, Japan) was employed to excite and detect fluorescence, and a spectrophotometer (PHARMACIA BIOTECH, Ultrospect 2000; England) to measure the ultraviolet (UV) and visible absorbance of the CDs. The morphology of the CDs was examined using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) (LEO 906, Zeiss, Germany). X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis of the CDs' pattern was conducted with a Bruker AXS D8 using Cu radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at 30 kV and 20 mA (TD-3700, China). Chemical elements were investigated using Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX). Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) and a zeta potential device assessed the CDs' size distribution and zeta potential using a Zetasizer (Ver. 7.11, MAL1032660, England). The functional groups present in the CDs were identified using Fourier-Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (TENSOR 27, Bruker, Germany).

A 15% (w/v) PCL/HPMC solution was prepared using 90% (v/v) acetic acid (AA), with a PCL/HPMC ratio of 70/30 (based on preliminary tests). The mixture was stirred for 2 h at 23 °C to achieve a smooth solution without clogging. Subsequently, Z-CD and TZ-CD were added to the solution at various concentrations (0%, 5%, and 15% w/w), followed by 5 min of sonication. The polymer solution was then subjected to a high voltage of 16 kV, with a 15 cm distance between the roller collector and the needle. The feeding rate for the solution was set at 1 mL per hour. The resulting electrospun PCL/HPMC nanofibrous films incorporating 5 wt% and 15 wt% Z-CD and TZ-CD were labeled as PCL/HPMC/Z-CD5%, PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD5%, PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15%, and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15%, respectively. The samples were maintained at 25 °C and 50% relative humidity.

The surface morphology of PCL/HPMC/CDs was examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (MIRA3 FEG-SEM, Czech) operating at a voltage of 15 kV after gold coating. The mean diameter of the nanofiber was determined by measuring random spots using ImageJ software. FTIR spectra of PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% were recorded with a spectrometer (Tensor27, Bruker Co., Ettlingen, Germany) in the wavelength range of 4000–500 cm−1 at a resolution of 4 cm−1 with 100 scans. The thermal stability of the fabricated NFs was assessed using a thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) device (TGA, Germany, Linseis, STA PT1600) under a nitrogen atmosphere, covering a temperature range of 25–600 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C per minute. The tensile properties of the NFs (4 cm × 1 cm) were evaluated using an Instron 5944 Universal Testing Machine (USA).

To assess the antioxidant activity of CDs, 60 μL of each CD was mixed with 360 μL of distilled water. Subsequently, 50 μL of these mixtures were combined with 4 mL of a 0.06 mM 2,2-diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) solution before being left in darkness for 1 h. The control sample was prepared by combining 50 μL of distilled water with 4 mL of DPPH.

To evaluate the antioxidant effectiveness of the NFs, 30 mg of each nanofiber was immersed in 4 mL of 10% (v/v) and 95% (v/v) ethanol for 24 h under agitation. Then, 0.5 mL of each nanofiber extract solution was mixed with 3 mL of DPPH. Finally, the mixture was kept in darkness for 1 h. The control sample was prepared by combining 3 mL of DPPH with 0.5 mL of NFs-free 10% (v/v) and 95% (v/v) ethanol. Afterward, the absorbance of the mixtures was measured at 517 nm using a spectrophotometer (Talib Al-Sudani et al., 2024). The antioxidant activity was determined using the following equation:
Free
radical
scavenging
activity
( % )
=
AC
−
AS
AC
× 100
Here, AC represents the absorbance of the control, and AS represents the absorbance of the sample.

For the determination of the minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of CDs against Escherichia coli (PTCC-1270) (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (PTCC-1015) (S. aureus) pathogens, a series of diluted CD samples (100 μL) was prepared in 96-well plates with concentrations ranging up to 8%, following a serial dilution procedure. The serially diluted Z-CD and TZ-CD solutions were supplemented with 100 μL of Mueller-Hinton broth (MHB) medium. Subsequently, each well was added 10 μL of bacterial broth with a turbidity of 0.5 McFarland (1.5 × 108 CFU/mL). The plates were then incubated for 20 h at 37 °C. The amount of bacterial growth in each well was measured using an ELISA microplate reader by assessing the change in optical density at 595 nm. The MIC was calculated using wells with considerably decreased optical density values (Ezati et al., 2022).

To examine the antimicrobial properties of NFs, UV light was employed to sterilize both surfaces of the NFs. The antibacterial activity of the NFs was evaluated using the total viable count (TVC) method against mesophilic and psychrotrophic bacteria. Initially, beef samples were sliced into uniform 5 g portions and homogenized in 45 mL of sterile 0.1% peptone water using a stomacher for 1 min. A series of dilutions (1:10) were prepared in a solution of 0.1% peptone water, and suitable dilutions of homogenates were plated. Plate count agar was employed to determine the TVC. Incubation periods were as follows: days 0, 1, 4, 8, and 12 at 30 °C and 4 °C for mesophilic and psychrotrophic bacteria, respectively. Microbiological counts were expressed as logarithms of colony-forming units per gram of sample (log CFU/g) (Takma & Korel, 2019).

To verify the antimicrobial properties of CDs and CD-added NFs, an oxygen radical absorbance capability (ORAC) assay was performed. This assay assessed the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) within the system by measuring the variation in fluorescence intensity of a 0.5 μg/mL fluorescein solution made alkaline by adding 50 μL of 1 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH). The change in fluorescence observed between solutions exposed to light on the surface of the CDs and NFs could be used to quantify the generated ROS. This quantification was achieved by applying the Stern-Volmer equation and validating the results by calibrating aliquots of 4, 8, 12, 16, and 20 mM H2O2 on the fluorescein solution for 1 h (R2 = 0.99). All fluorescence intensity measurements were conducted using a Biotek plate reader with an excitation filter at 485/20, an emission filter at 580/20, a gain set at 70, and a mirror position at 635 nm. To eliminate interference from the excitation wavelength, fluorescence intensity was measured using a filter at 580 ± 20 nm on the tail of the peak where the major emission peak of fluorescein was located at 515 nm (Ezati et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of total volatile basic nitrogen and thiobarbituric acid-reactive substances
ce:section.ce:para:
Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) and thiobarbituric acid-reactive substances (TBARS) tests were conducted following the procedure outlined by Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, and Ghasempour (2023), For TVB-N determination, 1 g of each beef sample was homogenized to measure TVB-N in 50 mL of a 3% MgO solution (v/w). The mixture was then distilled for 10 min using an automated Kjeldahl distillation apparatus (Koreatech, KR DIS-Auto, Korea) into 25 mL of a 4% boric acid solution. The pH was adjusted to 4.6 using 0.1 M sulfuric acid, and the amount of acid used was recorded. The TVB-N value was calculated using the formula:
TVB − N
( mg / 100 g
fresh
beef )
=
C × V × 14 × 100
W
In the formula, C represents the sulfuric acid concentration, V is the volume of sulfuric acid consumed, and W is the sample weight.

To assess TBARS levels, 1 g of each sample was homogenized with 10 mL of chloroform. Subsequently, 5 mL of TBRAS solution (0.187 g of thiobarbituric acid and 3.75 g of trichloroacetic acid, adjusted to a volume of 50 mL with 0.25 M HCl) was added, followed by 2 mL of a 2% BHT ethanolic solution. Afterward, the mixtures were then cooled for 20 min in a 90 °C water bath, followed by filtration using Whatman no. 1 filter paper. The absorbance was measured at 532 nm, and the TBARS quantity was determined using a malondialdehyde (MDA) standard curve at various concentrations (0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, and 2 mg/kg, R2 = 0.998).

The statistical analysis was applied to evaluate the effect of different CD amounts (0%, 5%, and 15%) on packaging fiber properties. Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) and Tukey's multiple tests, with a 95% confidence interval, were conducted using GraphPad Prism 9 software, with three replicates for each measurement.

An important feature of CDs is their fluorescence property. When Z-CD was excited at 480 nm in fluorimetry, the optimal spectrofluorometry emission occurred at 553 nm. Conversely, TZ-CD displayed the most pronounced spectrofluorometry emission at 541 nm, with an excitation wavelength of 490 nm (Fig. 1 A and B). It is important to note that the excitation wavelength influenced the maximum emission and intensity. As the excitation wavelength increased from 430 to 530 nm, as depicted in Fig. 1A and B, there was a slight shift in emission from 508 to 574 nm. This minor shift in the fluorescence peak was attributed to the narrow particle size distribution, indicating low-energy diffusion pathways. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that the fluorescence of TZ-CD exhibited a slight shift corresponding to the size's standard deviation determined by DLS results, which was lower than that of Z-CD (Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, & Ghasempour, 2023).

In Fig. 1C, the optical properties of CDs are illustrated. TZ-CDs displayed a prominent absorption peak at 252 nm, accompanied by a shoulder around 278 nm, whereas Z-CDs exhibited a single peak at 278 nm. The peak at 252 nm is attributed to the π−π* transition of C═C bands within localized sp2 clusters in the core, while the peak at 278 nm is associated with the n−π* transition of C═O bands of carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs (Wen et al., 2015). The TEM images presented in Fig. 1D and E depict the spherical and dispersed nature of the CDs. Fig. 1F shows the XRD pattern, revealing peaks at 2θ ≈ 23° for Z-CD and 2θ ≈ 25° for TZ-CD, indicating that both CDs possess an amorphous structure. Notably, previous studies have also documented the amorphous nature of CDs synthesized from natural sources, including orange peel, coriander leaves, corn powder, agricultural waste, paper, and roasted Atlantic salmon (Das Purkayastha et al., 2014; Prasannan & Imae, 2013; Sachdev & Gopinath, 2015; Song et al., 2019; Teymourinia, Salavati-Niasari, Amiri, & Safardoust-Hojaghan, 2017; Wei et al., 2014).

To assess the presence of elemental compounds, the EDX spectra of the CDs were examined (Ezati & Rhim, 2022). As illustrated in Fig. 1G and H, the C and O signals are attributed to the CDs, while the EDX spectra of N, S-co-doped CDs (TZ-CD) demonstrate the presence of N and S elements (Fig. 1H). These results validate the association of Z-CD with thiourea.

DLS results for CDs indicated that the sizes of Z-CD and TZ-CD were 3.96 ± 1.07 and 2.9 ± 0.42, respectively (Figs. S1A and S1B). These sizes are consistent with the expected size range for CD, which is typically below 10 nm. Zeta potential analysis, a reliable technique for assessing the net charge, indicated that Z-CD and TZ-CD have zeta potentials of −2.90 mV and −0.85 mV, respectively (Figs. S1C and S1D).

SEM technique was employed to analyze the morphology of developed NFs (Fig. 2 ). The average fiber diameters of PCL/HPMC, PCL/HPMC/Z-CD5%, PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD5%, PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15%, and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% were 216.69, 283.63, 282.19, 270.82, and 245.72 nm, respectively (Fig. 2). After increasing the CDs content (5% w/w), a significant increase (p < 0.05) in the diameter of nanofibers PCL/HPMC/Z-CD5%, PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD5% was observed compared to NFs without CDs (PCL/HPMC). On the other hand, with the increase of CD content (15% w/w), PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15%, and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% nanofibers' diameter decreased compared to fibers containing 5% CDs (Fig. 2). Ehtesabi and Massah (2021) similarly observed increased fiber diameter when incorporating CDs into NFs. This increase can be attributed to the alteration of polymer solution viscosity after adding CDs, which elevates viscosity and, consequently, the nanofiber diameter. Also, the decrease in diameter after adding more CDS (15% w/w) can be due to increased electrical conductivity in the polymer solution (Kiadeh, Ghaee, Pishbin, Nourmohammadi, & Farokhi, 2022; Rashidi et al., 2021). Additionally, SEM images of the NFs did not show any CD agglomerates or beads, indicating that the CDs were well-dispersed within the polymeric solution. Among the NFs containing CDs, the PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% samples exhibited uniform, bead-free structures and were consequently selected as optimal.

The strong mechanical properties of NFs play a critical role in their suitability for food packaging applications. The mechanical properties of NFs are presented in Table 1 . According to the results, the lowest and highest tensile values of NFs were, respectively, associated with PCL/HPMC (7.84 MPa) and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% (15.01 MPa) (Table 1). Notably, significantly higher stress levels were observed in NFs composed of PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% compared to those containing only PCL/HPMC (p < 0.05) (Table 1). These results underscored that NFs incorporating CDs exhibited improved tensile strength compared to CD-free nanofibers. Furthermore, increasing the percentage of CDs in PCL/HPMC enhanced tensile strength while decreasing elongation at break (EB).

Adding TZ-CD and Z-CD to the spinning mixture enhanced spinnability and promoted the stretching of NFs during processing. This enhancement in mechanical properties can be attributed to the effective interfacial interaction between the polymeric matrix and the CDs. Notably, the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups present on the surface of CDs facilitate interfacial interactions within the polymeric matrix, leading to an augmentation in mechanical strength (Ehtesabi & Massah, 2021). Notably, the tensile strength of NFs containing TZ-CD surpassed that of NFs with Z-CD. This disparity can be attributed to the size of the CDs. DLS and TEM results revealed that TZ-CD was smaller than Z-CD, providing a larger surface area and enhanced interfacial interactions with PCL/HPMC NFs (Kiadeh et al., 2022). Findings align with those reported by Ehtesabi and Massah (2021); Ezati et al. (2022); Kiadeh et al. (2022). Ezati et al. (2022) also observed that adding CDs to films reduced EB. Kousheh, Moradi, Tajik, and Molaei (2020) stated that the tensile strength of nanocellulose decreased while its EB increased using CDs at various concentrations. Likewise, Ezati et al. (2022) did not observe noteworthy alterations in the tensile strength of cellulose NFs following the addition of CDs. The variations in outcomes could be attributed to the diverse surface functional groups of CDs, as well as the concentration and specific type of CDs involved (Moradi et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant assay of CDs and NFs
ce:section.ce:para: The primary antioxidant mechanism of CDs involves electron transfer, hydrogen atom transfer, or a combination of both mechanisms (Supjaroenpisan & Paoprasert, 2021). As depicted in Fig. S7, the DPPH scavenging activity for Z-CD and TZ-CD was approximately 40.1 and 63.2%, respectively, with TZ-CD exhibiting the highest DPPH scavenging activity. Similarly, Li et al. (2019) reported a DPPH scavenging activity of 67.3% for N, S-co-doped CDs synthesized from green algae (Li et al., 2019).

The zeta potential of the CDs influences their antioxidant activity, and variations in zeta potentials are attributed to the electronegativity of the atoms used for doping the CDs (Ezati et al., 2022). In N-doped CDs, nitrogen with higher electronegativity neutralizes the surface electrons of CDs and decreases the negative zeta-potential value. Moreover, doping CDs with electronegative atoms such as nitrogen leads to the aggregation of positive charges on the surface of CDs, promoting interactions between CDs-DPPH and, thus, higher antioxidant activity (Chen et al., 2020). According to Shaik et al. (2022), doping CDs with Nitrogen or Sulfur increases their electron density, enhancing antioxidant activity (Shaik et al., 2022). Moreover, the surface of N, S-co-doped CDs contains abundant reducing groups such as O–H, CO, C–N, and C–S, which facilitate the reaction and reduction of DPPH free radicals (Li et al., 2019). While raw Zedo gum, used as a control, exhibited no antioxidant property, the increased antioxidant property of CDs derived from carbon sources can be ascribed to many hydrogen donor groups, particularly hydroxyl groups, on the surface of CDs (Zhao, Zhang, Wang, & Devahastin, 2021).

All NFs containing CDs demonstrated significantly higher antioxidant activity than CD-free NFs. The antioxidant activity of PCL/HPMC/Z-CD5%, PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15%, PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD5%, and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% was evaluated at 10 and 95% hydroethanolic solutions. In a 95% ethanolic solution, the antioxidant activity of PCL/HPMC/Z-CD5%, PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15%, PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD5%, and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% was approximately 32.7,76.1, 49.4, and 86.9%, respectively. In a 10% ethanolic solution, the antioxidant activity of NFs from PCL/HPMC/Z-CD5%, PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15%, PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD5%, and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% was about 34.7, 55.6, 42.5, and 80.5%, respectively (Fig. 3 ). The results revealed a dose-dependent increase in the scavenging property of the CD-NFs. In conclusion, PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% exhibited the highest antioxidant activity, while PCL/HPMC/Z-CD5% demonstrated the lowest activity in both ethanolic solutions. Another study also reported enhancing antioxidant properties by incorporating CDs into NFs (Pandey, Bankoti, Nath, & Dhara, 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activity of CDs and NFs
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC values for TZ-CD and Z-CD against E. coli were 0.5% and 1%, respectively, while against S. aureus, these values were 0.25% for TZ-CD and Z-CD samples. Additionally, the MBC values for TZ-CD and Z-CD against E. coli were 1% and 2%, respectively, and against S. aureus, they were both 0.5% (Fig. S8). In conclusion, TZ-CD displayed superior antibacterial activity compared to Z-CD.

The total viable count method was utilized to evaluate the antimicrobial activity of beef meat packaged with or without PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% NFs against mesophilic and psychrotrophic bacteria on days 0, 1, 4, 8, and 12 (Table 2 ). Unpackaged beef meat and meat packaged with PCL/HPMC were used as controls. Initially, all samples exhibited total viable counts of 5.14 and 4.97 log CFU/g for mesophilic and psychrotrophic bacteria, respectively. Beef meat packaged with PCL/HPMC showed microbial growth similar to unpackaged beef meat on days 1, 4, 8, and 12, indicating no antimicrobial activity. In contrast, beef meat packaged with PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% nanofiber displayed lower microbial growth of mesophilic and psychrotrophic bacteria than the controls. However, its initial total viable count for mesophilic bacteria increased to 5.18, 6.29, 7.62, and 8.25 log CFU/g on days 1, 4, 8, and 12, respectively. The initial total viable count for psychrotrophic bacteria increased to 5.09, 6.12, 7.41, and 8.08 log CFU/g on days 1, 4, 8, and 12, respectively. Similarly, beef meat packaged with PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% NF showed lower microbial growth of mesophilic and psychrotrophic bacteria compared to the controls, with an initial total viable count of 5.01 log CFU/g on day 1, which increased to 6.17, 7.34, and 8.12 log CFU/g on days 4, 8, and 12, respectively. The initial total viable count for psychrotrophic bacteria reduced to 4.95 log CFU/g on day 1 but then increased to 5.86, 7.13, and 7.94 log CFU/g on days 4, 8, and 12, respectively. In conclusion, the packaged samples exhibited slower microbial growth of mesophilic and psychrotrophic bacteria compared to the controls, with PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% NF demonstrating higher efficacy than PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15%.

The ROS-generating capacity of CDs and CD-loaded NFs was assessed at 0, 15, 30, 45, and 60 min to evaluate their antibacterial properties (Fig. S9). Alkaline fluorescein was applied to assess the production of green fluorescence and ROS-generating capacity of CDs. The fluorescein solution, used as a control, produced no ROS within 1 h. Z-CD generated ROS levels of 2.56 ± 0.7, 2.93 ± 0.6, 3.98 ± 0.1, 5.79 ± 0.9, and 7.68 ± 0.6 mM over the given time intervals. Meanwhile, TZ-CD produced ROS levels of 5.87 ± 1.5, 6.24 ± 0.2, 7.28 ± 0.8, 9.09 ± 0.5, and 10.98 ± 1.5 mM after 0, 15, 30, 45, and 60 min, respectively (Fig. S9A). Furthermore, PCL/HPMC, PCL/HPMC/Z5%, PCL/HPMC/TZ5%, PCL/HPMC/Z15%, and PCL/HPMC/TZ15% NFs generated ROS levels of 0.59 ± 0.09, 2.44 ± 0.61, 3.41 ± 0.31, 5.26 ± 0.57, and 7.54 ± 0.34 mM, respectively (Fig. S9B). It is evident that ROS generation increased over time, with TZ-CD displaying more significant ROS generation and, consequently, exhibiting higher antibacterial activity compared to Z-CD. In addition, PCL/HPMC/TZ5% and PCL/HPMC/TZ15% exhibited higher ROS generation and antibacterial activity than PCL/HPMC/Z5% and PCL/HPMC/Z15% due to the presence of TZ-CD and Z-CD. These results confirm the results of antimicrobial tests. Ezati et al. (2022) also found that films containing nitrogen-functionalized CDs produce more ROS than GCD.

According to Travlou et al. (2018), the antibacterial activity of CDs is influenced by the surface configuration of heteroatoms, surface charge (zeta potential), and particle size of the dots. Smaller CD sizes and higher surface-to-volume ratios contribute to bacterial membrane disruption and cell death (Travlou et al., 2018). Neutralized and negatively charged CDs display weaker antimicrobial properties than positively charged CDs (Ghorbani, Tajik, Moradi, Molaei, & Alizadeh, 2022). Cationic CDs interact with the negative charges of bacterial cells, whereas anionic and natural CDs repel them (Ezati et al., 2022). According to Ezati et al. (2022), doping CDs with boron, nitrogen, and sulfur atoms increased their antibacterial activity (Ezati et al., 2022).

The enhanced antibacterial properties of TZ-CD can be attributed to two factors: firstly, the electron-donating property of amides/amines on N-doped CDs results in the formation of active oxygen species (O2 −, OH−, and HO2 −) that adhere to the CD surfaces through electrostatic forces. These active oxygen species cause bacterial cell death by damaging lipids, nucleotides, proteins, and metabolites. Secondly, sulfonates and sulfonic acids, the primary functional groups of S-doped CDs, are considered potent electron-withdrawing agents (Travlou et al., 2018). Similarly, Raut, Patra, Mandal, Mandal, and Sahoo (2023) confirmed the antibacterial activity of N, S co-doped CDs against E.coli and S. aureus bacteria by disrupting the cell boundaries (Raut et al., 2023). Moreover, the results demonstrated that the tested CDs and NFs exhibited higher antibacterial activity against Gram-positive S. aureus, while their activity against Gram-negative E. coli was minimal. Similarly, other studies have shown that S- and N-doped CDs more efficiently inhibit the growth of Gram-positive bacteria than Gram-negative E. coli (Ezati et al., 2022; Travlou et al., 2018). This selective bactericidal activity can be attributed to differences in the cell wall structure of the two bacterial strains and their affinity for interaction with the surface functional groups of the dots. Gram-positive bacteria with no outer membrane, are more sensitive to reactive oxygen species (Ezati et al., 2022).

TVB-N is a widely recognized biochemical marker for monitoring meat spoilage (Holman et al., 2021). The National Food Safety Standard (2017b) states that fresh beef and other livestock meat products should have TVB-N values below 15 mg/100 g (Holman et al., 2021). The Iran Veterinary Organization also has set an acceptable limit of 28 mg/100 g for TVB-N (Sayadi, Mojaddar Langroodi, & Jafarpour, 2021). TVB-N results are illustrated in Fig. 4 A. The initial TVB-N value in all examined samples was 4.23 mg/100 g, gradually increasing during 14 days (Fig. 4A). The increase in TVB-N levels during storage can be attributed to the proliferation of microorganisms and the breakdown of non-protein nitrogenous compounds, such as nucleic acids and protein components, due to the release of proteolytic enzymes (Alirezalu et al., 2021). All samples maintained TVB-N values within the fresh meat range until the fourth day. After six days, the TVB-N values for uncoated beef and beef coated with PCL/HPMC exceeded the fresh meat range, while for beef coated with PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% NFs, this occurred after eight days. Furthermore, the TVB-N values for beef coated with PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% NFs surpassed the acceptable limit after 10 and 11 days, respectively.

Notably, beef coated with PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% NFs consistently exhibited significantly lower TVB-N levels than the other samples. This can be attributed to CDs' antimicrobial and antioxidative properties. Generally, CDs delay the growth of bacteria and exhibit inhibitory effects against lipid oxidation, thereby reducing the formation of TVB-N (Alirezalu et al., 2021; Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, & Ghasempour, 2023). These findings align with the results obtained from the antioxidant and antimicrobial tests. Previous research also demonstrated that pork covered with CD-containing chitosan film produced significantly less TVB-N than uncoated samples after nine days of storage, primarily due to CDs' antibacterial properties (Lin et al., 2022). For similar reasons, Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, and Ghasempour (2023) also reported that fish covered with a film containing CDs produced less TVB-N after 12 days of storage than uncoated samples and films without CDs.

The TBARS test assesses lipid peroxidation levels, reflecting the presence of compounds, primarily aldehydes and ketones, generated during secondary lipid oxidation. The accumulation of oxidation byproducts can negatively impact the sensory quality of meat, leading to a rancid aroma and flavor (Sayadi et al., 2021). As shown in Fig. 4B, TBARS values gradually increased for all beef samples during 14 days.

The TBARS levels of beef coated with PCL/HPMC, PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15%, and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% NFs were 1.55, 1.13, and 1.27 mg MDA/kg, respectively, demonstrating lower TBARS formation compared to uncoated beef (Fig. 4B). The reduced TBARS in PCL/HPMC NFs can be attributed to their ability to restrict oxygen diffusion on the sample surface. Conversely, the potential antioxidant properties of CDs may contribute to diminished oxidation in PCL/HPMC/TZ-CD15% and PCL/HPMC/Z-CD15% NFs (Alirezalu et al., 2021; Khoshkalampour, Ghorbani, & Ghasempour, 2023). These findings were consistent with the results of antioxidant tests. Variations in TBARS levels across different samples may be related to the degradation of malondialdehyde at the third stage, influenced by interactions with meat constituents such as amino acids, proteins, and glucose (Khoshkalampour, Ahmadi, Ghasempour, Lim, & Ghorbani, 2023).

According to the results, the TBARS values for all beef samples remained below 1.75 mg MDA/kg throughout the storage period. The critical sensory perception threshold for detecting meat rancidity is 2 mg MDA/kg (Mehdizadeh, Tajik, Langroodi, Molaei, & Mahmoudian, 2020).

The electrospinning technique was employed to produce nanosystems, integrating biodegradable PCL and natural-based HPMC with carbon quantum dots. Nano CDs were successfully synthesized from the insoluble fraction of Zedo gum, a waste of natural resources, displaying enhanced fluorescent properties and remarkable antioxidant activity compared to the gum solution. These CDs were doped with heteroatoms to enhance their functionality, and N- and S-co-doped CDs exhibited superior characteristics. Incorporating CDs led to improved mechanical properties and thermal stability, as well as antioxidant and antimicrobial activity. N, S-co-doped CDs exhibited superior functional properties compared to bare CDs. Using NFs loaded with CDs effectively extended the shelf life of meat samples, making them ideal and innovative components for active packaging, especially for items susceptible to oxidation and bacterial spoilage, such as meat. Nevertheless, it is crucial to consider specific limitations associated with using CDs as active fillers in packaging NFs. Modification is necessary to enhance the functional properties of CDs. CDs obtained from natural sources using chemicals for modification can lead to toxicity of CDs. Therefore, the toxicity of NFs integrated with CDs should be carefully investigated.

Shaghayegh Ahmadi: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ali Khoshkalampour: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Marjan Ghorbani: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Soghra Ramezani: Conceptualization, Methodology. Zahra Ghasempour: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Nazila Ghareaghajlou: Methodology, Writing – original draft.
